topics.name: Gene expression data are highly dimensional: there are

chunks.content: many thousands of measurements made from a small number of samples. Descriptive (exploratory) statistics help you to find meaningful patterns in the data. A first step is to arrange the data in a matrix. Next, use a distance metric to define the relatedness of the different data points. Two commonly used distance metrics are: -- Euclidean distance -- Pearson coefficient of correlation Analysis part II: Descriptive statistics

Euclidian distance: In 3D space, the distance between two points (x 1 , x 2 , x 3 ) and (y 1 , y 2 , y 3 ) is d xy = [ (x 1 -y 1 ) 2 + (x 2 -y 2 ) 2 + (x 3 -y 3 ) 2 ] 1/2 In n-dimensional space, the distance is d xy = [  i=1 to n (x i -y i ) 2 ] 1/2 Numerical values are often normalized and variance corrected prior to calculating distances, e.g. transformed variable x’ has mean=0, st.dev.=1.

topics.name: For any two series of numbers {X

r varies from r = 1 (two series correlate exactly)  to r = -1 (inverse correlation). r = 0 indicates X and Y are uncorrelated. This can be translated to a rough distance measure using D = 1 - r. (X i -X avg )  X (Y i -Y avg )  Y

chunks.content: of gene expression data. Genes may be clustered, or samples, or both. Hierarchical clustering is often used - This may be agglomerative (building up the branches of a tree, beginning with the two most closely related objects) or divisive (building the tree by finding the most dissimilar groups first). In each case, we end up with a tree having branches and nodes.

12 3 4 5 12 6 45 7 3 8 9 UPGMA: unweighted pair group method with arithmetic mean.  At each iteration, closest two groups are combined to form one new group. This process is continued until there is only one group, producing a rooted tree. UPGMA may be a suitable method for expression data, as expression data isn’t typically additive and UPGMA doesn’t assume additivity.

•Data are classified into k groups, where k is specified by the user •useful if expected number of clusters in known (e.g. 3 treatments) •One method: - pick K objects (genes) at random - all other genes assigned to a group based on distance to initial K genes - center of each cluster is defined using a distance metric - genes are iteratively reassigned to clusters to minimize the within-cluster sum of squared distances - converges after many iterations - objects within a cluster contain similar expression profiles

each object (gene) is initially assigned a group (random or seeded) members are iteratively reassigned until convergence If K genes are randomly selected, then all others initially assigned based on distance to the initial K genes: Genes are iteratively reassigned based on group average, until convergence (no further changes at each step)
Like k-means clustering, but initial assignment is not random One chooses a geometry of 'nodes'-for example, a 3x2 grid http://www.genome.wi.mit.edu/MPR/SOM.html

topics.name: The nodes are mapped into high dimensional space,

chunks.content: and objects assigned to nodes. The node locations are iteratively adjusted, along with object assignments.

Unlike k-means clustering, which is unstructured, SOMs allow one to impose partial structure on the clusters. The principle of SOMs is as follows. One chooses an initial geometry of “nodes” such as a 3 x 2 rectangular grid (indicated by solid lines in the figure connecting the nodes). Hypothetical trajectories of nodes as they migrate to fit data during successive iterations of SOM algorithm are shown. Data points are represented by black dots, six nodes of SOM by large circles, and trajectories by arrows.

topics.name: Ying et al. (2016). Biological Research. 49. 10. CC BY 4.0
Inferential statistics are used to make inferences

chunks.content: about a population from a sample. Hypothesis testing is a common form of inferential statistics. A null hypothesis is stated, such as: “There is no difference in signal intensity for the gene expression measurements in normal and diseased samples.” The alternative hypothesis is that there is a difference. We use a test statistic to decide whether to accept or  reject the null hypothesis. Using traditional statistics, a significance level  is often set to p <  = 0.05.

-Say you take a measure the expression of a single gene in two populations. The null hypothesis is that the expression is the same: H 0 : μ 1 = μ 2 ,  where: H 0 = the null hypothesis μ 1 = the mean of sample type 1, and μ 2 = the mean of sample type 2. -The alternative hypothesis is H A : μ 1 ≠ μ. If you can show that the null hypothesis is unlikely, then the alternative hypothesis is likely to be true.

p-values can be used to estimate type I error, the chance of a false positive. So, if a p-value is reported as less than a threshold α (say p < α = 0.05), it means the chance of a false positive (incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis) is less than 5%. Therefore, there is <5% chance of ‘crying wolf’.

p-values and the null distribution The null distribution is the expected distribution if no sample effects are present, just the expected variation in expression for each gene. The p-value is the probability of seeing the observed effect under the null hypothesis. If two groups of samples were randomly selected from a null distribution and tested, and the test was repeated many times we would expect an even distribution of p-values. i.e. if we sampled two groups from a null distribution 1000 times and estimated p-values, approximately 50 p-values would be between 0 and 0.05. Null or Background distribution P-value distribution 0 1

If real effects are present, the distribution of real effects should be different than the null distribution. If the effects are large enough, corresponding p-values will be very small. If we don’t know which effects are real, the resulting distribution of p-values will include points from both null and alternative distributions. Say in our experiment we test 1000 genes, and 50 of these are differentially expressed (from the alternative distribution).  Using a p-value cutoff of 0.05 we might detect most of the true positives, but also include many false positives. Null and alternative distributions p-value distribution 950 samples from null distribution, 50 from alternate distribution 01 p-values and the null distribution

Is it appropriate to set the significance level to p < 0.05 for high-throughput data? If you hypothesize that one specific gene is up-regulated, you can set the probability value to 0.05. Within a transcriptomics experiment, you might measure the expression of 10,000 genes and hope that any of them are up- or down-regulated. But you would expect to see 5% (500 genes) identified at the p < 0.05 level by chance alone. A very strict option is to apply a Bonferroni correction. The level for statistical significance is divided by the number of measurements, and the criterion becomes: p < (0.05)/10,000   or   p < 5 x 10 -6

The Bonferroni correction means that the chance of any false positive being present is < 0.05. However, using a Bonferroni correction for multiple hypotheses will reject many true positive cases. p < (0.05)/10,000   or   p < 5 x 10 -6 This has led to widespread use of another measure, the False Discovery Rate, or FDR. Instead of setting a family-wise error rate, the rate of false discoveries is set to a given value. FDR = Expected (# false positives / # total positives)

correction will set the number of expected false positives to some fraction of all detected positives, e.g. q = 0.10. Here, approximately 10% of all ‘calls’ (TP+FP) will be false positives. A threshold p T can be set based on the observed p-values. The value of p T will typically be << 0.05. P-value distribution 00.010.060.020.050.040.03 Expected TN p T Expected FP Expected TP

-Whole genome shotgun sequencing uses reads sampled from all chromosomes of a given genome -Reads are typically short read (100-250 bp) sequences collected via high-throughput sequencing, but may also include longer sequences from Nanopore or PacBio sequencing -Assembly is possible through over-sampling, where reads will overlap with other reads and permit assembly -De novo sequence assembly is the reconstruction of sequence up to chromosome length, without reference to an existing genome or transcriptome sequence assemblies

Assembly issues... -General assumption is that similar overlapping reads originate from the same location in the genome. This permits joining of reads into contigs (contiguous sequences) -However, genome sequences typically contain repeat regions, ranging from short repeats to two or more regions of thousands of base pairs with nearly identical sequence -Errors in sequencing will also occur, and may appear similar to SNPs or other genome features -Coverage of the genome will also vary, resulting in some regions with high coverage and others with low or no coverage

-Available technologies have different error structures and read lengths, which will influence assembly methods -paired-end reads (or ‘mate-pairs’) can help resolve local repeats. Paired end reads are sequenced from either end of a longer sequence, say 100 bp from each end of an 700 bp segment, or 100 bp from each end of a 180 bp segment -Short jump reads are similar to paired-end reads, except they are of greater total length (say ~3500 bp) and are derived from circularized DNA fragments
-Greedy -Overlap-layout-consensus -De Bruijn graph

-Assembler makes choice with greatest immediate benefit, such as joining best overlapping reads if consistent with existing assembly -Emphasizes local assembly -Uses heuristics to avoid misassembly of repeats -Examples include early assemblers (TIGR Assembler, Phrap) and some recent tools (VCAKE)

-Identifies all pairs of reads which overlap sufficiently well -Creates a graph with a node for each read and edges connecting overlapping reads -May have high computational overhead for paired overlap calculations. Requires effective indexing or other methodology to circumvent this -Celera assembler, SGA

-Uses exact substrings of length k -Creates a graph of kmers that overlap by k-1 letters -Typically uses reads to refine graph structure and remove inconsistencies -Better results if errors corrected prior to assembly -Velvet, SOAPdenovo, ALLPATHS

topics.name: How does assembly using De Bruijn graphs actually work?

chunks.content: Collect sequence data, say 2 x 200 paired end reads. Sequencing depth should be sufficient, say ~40x on average. Split all reads into k-mers, say length 31. The number of possible kmers k = 4^31, or ~4 x 10 18 (4 billion billion) These kmers are long enough that the large majority should be unique within the genome. If they are unique, it is possible to put kmers into long ‘chains’ or contigs. Debruijn graphs and kmers

If the genome was random sequence with no repeats, then the De Bruijn graph would be a single long chain (assuming all regions of the genome were sequenced, and there were no errors in sequencing). But, there are errors and there are repeated regions. An error near the end of a read could result in the De Bruijn graph looking something like this:

11 25 23 29 Here, the numbers represent how often the kmer was seen (how many reads it appears in). The errors are relatively rare so they show up as low numbers. GCTGAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGA  28 CTGAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGAT  26 TGAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGATC  25 TGAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGATG   1 GAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGATGT   1 GAAAATCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGAGATCT  23

Repeats cause a different issue. Here, the kmers map to more than one location in the genome.  The De Bruijn graph will contain a ‘join’, where two chains representing different parts of the genome join together:

61 22 To resolve this into two separate paths, we need information from sequences that are longer than the kmer length (reads or paired-end reads). GCTGCCCGGCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGTTT  23 ACTGCCCGGCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGTTT  37 CTGCCCGGCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGTTTG  61 TGCCCGGCGGTGGGTGATAGCAAGAGTTTGC  63 373735 63 many kmers later...

Three methods to resolve graph complexity. (a) Read threading joins paths across repeats that are shorter than read lengths. (b) Mate threading joins paths across repeats shorter than paired-end distances. (c) Path following chooses the path if length fits paired-end constraint. If it is not possible to select the correct path, the graph can be ‘cut’ into multiple contigs (path not resolved)

topics.name: Nagarajan and Pop, Nature reviews genetics 2013

chunks.content: In the above figure, our kmer chains have a repeat ‘R’, and from the De Bruijn graph we don’t know whether paths (ARB and CRD) or (ARD and CRB) are correct. Read r1 supports path CRD, and paired end reads r5-r6 support path ARB. Based on this (and information from other reads) this would be split into two paths ARB and CRD.

For a kmer length of 50 in E. coli, the unresolved graph looks like this: Read data and paired end data would be used to resolve this into a set of linear paths (ideally one path, though that may not be possible).
A competing method is OLC (Overlap Layout Consensus). Here, you need to identify which reads overlap. If there are 100 million reads, this is very computationally intensive as there are ~ 10 8 x

10 8 pairs. Heuristic (approximate) methods are needed to speed up overlap calculations, but can also introduce errors (e.g. incorrectly identify repeat overlaps). A De bruijn graph removes the need to identify pairwise overlaps, and instead uses kmer counting and ordering. It identifies the ‘simple’ paths, and then the more complicated patterns (repeats etc) are resolved using data from complete reads. OLC and De Bruijn methods are both in use, and both can produce good assembly results. So... why use a De Bruijn graph anyway?

topics.name: Some of the general features of eukaryotes that distinguish

chunks.content: them from prokaryotes are: • eukaryotes include many multicellular organisms, in addition to unicellular organisms. • eukaryotes have [1] a membrane-bound nucleus, [2] intracellular organelles, and [3] a cytoskeleton • Most eukaryotes undergo sexual reproduction • The genome size of eukaryotes spans a wider range than that of most prokaryotes • Eukaryotic genomes have a lower density of genes • Prokaryotes are haploid; eukaryotes have varying ploidy • Eukaryotic genomes tend to be organized into linear chromosomes with a centromere and telomeres.

why eukaryotic genome sizes vary C value = haploid genome size. The range in C values does not correlate well with the complexity of the organism. This phenomenon is called the C value paradox. The solution to this “paradox” is that genomes are filled with large tracts of noncoding, often repetitive DNA sequences that don’t code for protein, but still might have functional roles in chromosome structure or gene regulation .

Humans have ~20,000 genes; sequenced plant and animal genomes typically have ~12,000 to 30,000 genes -About 2-3% of the human genome codes for proteins ->980 million variants have been identified in the human genome (NCBI dbSNP, build 155) -A random pair of haploid genomes differs at a rate of 1 base pair every 800 on average in aligned regions. Fewer than 1% of SNPs alter protein sequence -Segmental duplications (a duplicated region with one or more similar copies elsewhere in the genome) are common The human genome project

-Although the focus of genome annotation is often to identify protein coding genes regions, other features are important. Genome annotation can include -General genome features such as GC content -Assembly quality measures -Protein coding regions (~2-3% in mammals) -Gene structure: introns, exons, UTRs, alternative splicing -tRNAs and rRNAs -snoRNAs, lncRNAs, other RNAs -Repetitive regions -Mobile elements -Variants -Regulatory elements (harder to characterize) Genome annotation

topics.name: Five main classes of repetitive DNA

chunks.content: [1] interspersed repeats (transposon-derived) [2] processed pseudogenes [3] simple sequence repeats (micro-, minisatellites) [4] segmental duplications [5] blocks of tandem repeats (e.g. at centromeres)

1. Interspersed repeats Interspersed repeats (transposon-derived repeats) constitute ~45% of the human genome.  They involve RNA intermediates (retroelements) or DNA intermediates (DNA transposons). DNA transposons (3% of human genome) RNA-mediated: •Long interspersed nuclear elements (LINEs, ~21%); these encode a reverse transcriptase. •Short interspersed nuclear elements (SINEs, ~13%); these include Alu repeats •Retrovirus-like elements -Long-terminal repeat transposons (LTRs, ~8%) •LINE-1 (17% of human genome) and SINEs are active in the human genome, but at a low level (1 new L1 per ~20-200 births)

There are 300,000 Alu repeats in the human genome. These are about 300 base pairs and contain an AluI restriction enzyme site. They occupy 3% of the genome. Their distribution is non-random: they are retained in GC-rich regions and may confer some benefit. Interspersed repeats – Alu elements

topics.name: Examples include retrotransposed genes that lack introns,

chunks.content: such as: ADAM20 NM_003814  14q (original gene on 8p) Cetn1  NM_004066  18p (original gene on Xq) Glud2  NM_012084  Xq (original gene on 10q) Pdha2  NM_005390  4q (original gene on Xp)

2. Processed pseudogenes Processed pseudogenes: These genes have a stop codon or frameshift mutation and do not encode a functional protein. They can arise from retrotransposition, or following gene duplication and subsequent gene loss. An on-line resource is available at Mark Gerstein’s website, http://www.pseudogene.org

3. Simple sequence repeats Simple sequence repeats (SSRs): Microsatellites: from one to a dozen base pairs Examples: (A) n , (CA) n , (CGG) n These may be formed by replication slippage. Minisatellites: a dozen to 500 base pairs Simple sequence repeats of a particular length and composition occur preferentially in different species. Micro- and minisatellites comprise 3% of the genome. In humans, an expansion of triplet repeats such as CAG is associated with at least 14 disorders, including Huntington’s disease.

4. Segmental duplication Segmental duplications: These are blocks of about 1 kilobase to 300 kb that are copied intra- or interchromosomally. Duplicated regions often share very high (99%) sequence identity. Later work has demonstrated copy number variation is a major source of variability in the human genome As an example, consider a group of lipocalin genes on human chromosome 9.
gene duplications (after Lacazette et al., 2000)

The initial estimates in the 2001 human genome papers were that about 3.6% of the finished human genome sequence may represent segmental duplications, with lengths typically 10-50 kb. This has since been revised and is now closer to 5-10%.

topics.name: Five main classes of repetitive DNA

chunks.content: 5. Blocks of tandem repeats These include telomeric repeats (e.g. TTAGGG in humans) and centromeric repeats (e.g. a 171 base pair repeat of  satellite DNA in humans). Such repetitive DNA can span millions of base pairs, and are often species-specific.

topics.name: Dinucleotides of CpG are under-represented in

chunks.content: genomic DNA, occuring at one fifth the expected frequency. CpG dinucleotides are often methylated on cytosine (and subsequently may be deaminated to thymine). Methylated CpG residues are often associated with house-keeping genes in the promoter and exonic regions. Methyl-CpG binding proteins recruit histone deacetylases and are thus responsible for transcriptional repression. They have roles in gene silencing, genomic imprinting, and X-chromosome inactivation. Broad genomic landscape: CpG islands

50,267 CpG islands in human genome 28,890 after masking repeats with RepeatMasker 5-15 CpG islands per megabase (for comparison, there are about <40 genes per megabase) Broad genomic landscape: CpG islands

It is essential to identify repetitive DNA in eukaryotic genomes. Commonly used databases of known repeats include RepBase, with consensus repeats and low-complexity regions, and Dfam, containing profile HMMs of RepBase repeats. RepeatMasker (https://www.repeatmasker.org/) is a program that searches DNA queries against RepBase, classifying repeats by type and optionally masking the input genome by replacing repeat regions with Ns or Xs. RepeatMasker is available for download and through many websites. RepeatMasker searches are performed using several popular search engines including nhmmer, cross_match, ABBlast/WUBlast, RMBlast and Decypher.

• HMMs are probabilistic models based on state transitions in a sequence • HMMs can be similar to profiles based on multiple sequence alignments, but are more versatile and can include gap probabilities • can be used to: -represent a profile of a group of related genes or proteins -define changes in states in genes, such as intron/exon boundaries -identify changes in state in proteins, such as inside → transmembrane helix →outside for transmembrane proteins -Codes for a sequence pattern than can be used for gene or protein identification and classification

topics.name: A simple ‘toy’ HMM for splice site recognition

chunks.content: • This is a three-state model, with states for exon (X), transition (5), and intron (I). • In model, bases have an equal probability within exons (A=C=G=T=25%), the transition state is nearly always G(95%) but can be A(5%), and the intron state is AT rich (A=T=40%; G=C=10%) • The most probable state is found by the Viterbi algorithm (a dynamic programming algorithm similar to NW and SW).

chunks.content: •Likelihood Theory based on work of Fisher (1922) •proposed for molecular data by Felsenstein (1973) •uses a model of sequence evolution –what is the probability of obtaining the data (sequence alignment) given the model (tree and model of evolution) –P(data|tree,model) “probability of observing the data, given a tree and evolutionary model” •another way of saying this: –given a model of sequence evolution, find the tree and parameters which maximizes the likelihood of the observed data (alignment) –a model is always implicit when considering the probability of events

topics.name: Given a system, probability is the set

chunks.content: of possible results given the model. For example, if you flip a coin ten times and there is a 50% chance of heads (p=0.50), then you get a distribution of possible results. Likelihood is based on observed results – for example, if you observe 7 of 10 heads, likelihood estimates the parameters, e.g. what is the likelihood of p(Heads) = 0.50?

Probability is the predicted result, based on an assumed model - e.g. what is the probability of 6 of 10 heads in a coin toss, based on the model P(heads) = 0.5? Likelihood predicts the model (parameters) based on an observed set of results. - e.g. what is the likelihood of P(heads) = 0.5, given that we have observed 6 of 10 heads in a coin toss?

•model of evolution –how is your data going to behave? •JC, Tamura-Nei, GTR, etc. –parameters: transitions/transversions, nucleotide changes •Tree –we determine the likelihood for a subset of trees (heuristic) and output the ‘best tree’ –local optimum (unless doing an exhaustive search)
•Because ML assumes independence of sites, the total likelihood is the product of the likelihoods of each column in the alignment •For example, take two short sequences, GATCG and GAACA

•For a given time period, say the probability of a nucleotide change = 0.05 •transitions and transversion probabilities are the same (e.g. P A>G = αt = 0.05), nucleotide frequencies  (Π A , Π G ,Π C ,Π T ) are included •Probability matrix is this •The likelihood of a sequence changing from GATCG to GAACA can be calculated: A       G      C      T A G C T

•The example on the previous slide is going from one sequence to another •When you have more than 3 sequences, each term in the likelihood calculation is expanded to incorporate tree topologies and branch lengths •It becomes a very large calculation A B C 0.1 0.2 0.5

topics.name: Trees are part of the model as well

chunks.content: •Branch lengths, or time of evolutionary divergence, have an influence on the likelihood values –models of base pair evolution can incorporate this information •The probability of base pair changes on a very short branch length is low •extremely long branch lengths eventually see a reduction in the likelihood –multiple substitutions at the same site

•Maximum likelihood doesn’t require a specific algorithm – it compares trees but doesn’t generate them. •There are a variety of algorithms for generating trees for testing •Most algorithms utilize a method where new trees are generated from previous trees, such as a ‘branch swapping’ algorithm

•In general, character-based methods such as maximum likelihood do not provide a way to build trees, only to compare them •Other methods are needed to generate new trees to rank and compare (e.g shorter branch lengths in an evolutionary model, or a better fit with the observed data) •A generalized approach: 1.Build a starting tree using a fast method (NJ, UPGMA, etc) 2.Make changes to that tree by moving branches or nodes 3.Over time, keep trees that have an improved score
•Each internal branch is connected to four groups within a tree •NNI changes how an internal branch is connected (three possibilities total)
•If the score is better, keep the new tree (or alternately, keep the new tree with some probability, based on the score) Image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:NNI.svg
and regrafting (SPR) •A subtree is cut from the original tree and moved to a new location •i.e. the pruned subtree is added to a different branch
•As with other methods, if the score is better, keep the new tree (or keep the new tree with some probability, based on the score) Image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:SPR.svg
•Starting tree is split into two •All edge combinations for subtrees are recombined, generating a set of new trees
•As with other methods, if the score is better, keep a new tree (or keep a new tree with some probability, based on the score) Image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:TBR.svg

Lastly, there is the Parsimony Ratchet... •One issue with tree exploration is that all methods may get stuck in ‘local minima’ – there may be a better answer, but you can’t get there from here. The parsimony ratchet uses a bootstrap-like approach: 1.Get a reasonable starting tree 2.Re-weight a subset (10-20%) of columns in your MSA 3.Explore new tree space (using TBR or another method) and keep best tree 4.Reset to original weights and explore tree space again 5.Keep the best tree so far. 6.Repeat steps 2-5  (100 times or more)

topics.name: Phylogenetic reconstruction would be relatively simple, if:

chunks.content: • a constant rate of mutation was seen in all branches • sequences are only moderately divergent, so multiple changes are not seen at individual sites Unfortunately, neither of these conditions hold for most data sets.

topics.name: The following should apply to all reconstructions:

chunks.content: • an accurate multiple sequence alignment is used • an appropriate model of evolution is selected • for species trees, aligned sequences are true orthologs Ideally, - reconstruction method should be appropriate for the data - different models of evolution are compared - quality of the tree is assessed

topics.name: Possible factors in models of molecular evolution:

chunks.content: • different substitution preferences - nucleotide transitions vs. transversions - amino acid substitution patterns • different rates at different sequence positions - different codon positions (1st, 2nd, 3rd) - different degrees of conservation • different rates on different branches of the tree

topics.name: The simplest model is the fractional alignment distance,

chunks.content: or p-distance: p =  D / L, where D is the number of observed changes L is the length of the sequence Problems with this measure: - for short sequence lengths, statistical variation in p-distance is high - does not account for multiple substitutions - does not account for different rates between sequences

p accounts for multiple substitutions at individual sites.  Assuming there is a fixed rate of mutation per unit time, the probability of one position changing is       p 1/2 =  1 - e -rt , and one of two aligned positions changing is  p =  1 - e -2rt . If distance is defined as d = 2rt, then  p =  1 - e -d , rearranging and solving for d,   d p = - ln (1 - p) where d p is the Poisson distance. - this agrees with p-distance if there aren’t many changes, say p ≲ 0.2. The Poisson distance d p gets increasing larger as p-distance gets closer to one.

topics.name: For any nucleotide model, the goal is for the model to

chunks.content: effectively represent nucleotide changes within a set of sequences. There is no one ‘best’ model for all sequence data sets however - the constraints on different sequences will vary, and different models may work best with different data sets. For example, very small data sets might need a simple model, whereas a large data set that is highly functionally constrained may need a more complicated model.

topics.name: Nucleotide models can be very simple, or have many

chunks.content: parameters: Jukes and Cantor single parameter model Kimura Two parameter model Tamura model, Multi-parameter Typically, a number of models are considered and the one that provides the best match with the data is selected.

The Jukes-Cantor model is a simple model for nucleotide sequence evolution. Assumptions: - all sites are independent - rates of evolution are the same at all sites - all substitutions are equally likely, and occur at rate  Per unit time, the chance of a given change (e.g. A -> T) is , and the chance of a nucleotide not changing is (1 - 3). The change per unit time can be represented as a ‘rate matrix’: and the distance d JC can be derived as d JC =  -3/4 ln [ 1 - (4/3) p] where p is the p-distance =  D / L AGCT A-3 G-3 C-3 T-3

topics.name: Aside: Markov processes, Matrices and Rates

chunks.content: Models that have independent sites and parameters that do not change over time can be represented as time-continuous Markov model. The Jukes-Cantor Markov model and corresponding transition probability matrix P(t) are shown below.

topics.name: Aside: Markov processes, Matrices and Rates
At t = 0, P(0) = I , the identity

chunks.content: matrix. The rate matrix Q is the rate of change of P(t), where P(∆t) = P(0) + Q ∆t and  P(t+∆t) = P(t) + Q ∆t for ∆t → 0. Q provides a concise and invariant description of the evolutionary model.

topics.name: Other models account for additional factors

chunks.content: The Kimura two-parameter model (K2P) uses different rates for transitions  (A ⇄ G, C ⇄ T) and transversions  (A ⇄ C, A ⇄ T, C ⇄ G, G ⇄ T) . Transversions are expected to occur at a lower rate. AGCT A-2- G-2- C-2- T-2- Here, the corrected distance can be calculated as d K2P =  - 1/2 ln(1 - 2P -Q) - 1/4 ln(1 - 2Q) where P is the observed number of aligned transitions and Q is the observed number of aligned transversions. (note total probability of change is p = P+Q)

topics.name: Other models account for additional factors

chunks.content: Both the Jukes-Cantor and K2P model assume a 1:1:1:1 ratio of nucleotide composition. This can be corrected for as well, as in the HKY85 model (Hasegawa, Kishino, and Yano, 1985). correcting for different rates at different positions These models still don’t account for different rates at different positions (say third position within a codon vs. a highly conserved site). This can be corrected using a Gamma distribution, which models variation across different sequence positions. This can be applied to a variety of different models, and a corresponding distance measure, the Gamma distance, d  (Uzell and Corbin, 1971) can be calculated. For example, for the Jukes-cantor model, d JC =  -3/4 ln [ 1 - (4/3)p] becomes  d JC+ =   3/4 a([ 1 - (4/3)p] -1/a -1) where a is the gamma parameter.

topics.name: Protein sequence data are often in the range a = 0.2 to 3.5

chunks.content: Similar to the previously described nucleotide models, models have been generated for protein sequence data. Common practice is to use empirically derived substitution matrices, similar to the PAM and BLOSUM matrices. Commonly used matrices for phylogenetic studies include JTT (Jones, Taylor, Thornton, 1992), WAG (Whelan and Goldman, 2001), and LG (Le and Gascuel, 2008).

So... which is the most accurate tree?
In the previous example, different values of the gamma

chunks.content: parameter resulted in different trees. Some features are present in all the trees, but branch lengths and some groupings are different. Fortunately, there is a ‘best’ choice for the gamma distribution – the value that best fits the data, i.e. one that best represents the observed variability within the given set of sequences.

topics.name: For our substitution models, there is no universal model

chunks.content: that will work best with all data sets, as the parameter weights and which parameters to include is dependent on the data. Too few parameters can lead to inaccuracy, convergence upon the wrong tree (inconsistency) Too many parameters can reduce statistical power, the ability to reject an hypothesis. Which is the best model to use?

Fortunately, it is possible to compare the models and determine which is the best quality. Here, ‘quality’ is a balance between goodness-of-fit of the model and model complexity. Many phylogenetic analysis programs will contain algorithms to assess models (e.g. Modeltest and Prottest for comparing nucleotide and protein data respectively). This is done using some measure of quality, such as the AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) or BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion). Which is the best model to use?

At the molecular level, evolution is a process of mutation with selection. Molecular evolution is the study of changes in genes and proteins throughout different branches of the tree of life. Phylogeny is the inference of evolutionary relationships. Traditionally, phylogeny relied on the comparison of morphological features between organisms. Today, most phylogenies are constructed using molecular sequence data. Introduction

topics.name: By the 1950s, it became clear that amino acid substitutions

chunks.content: occur non-randomly. For example, Sanger  and colleagues noted that most amino acid changes in the insulin A chain are restricted to a disulfide loop region. Such differences are expected to be “neutral”changes (Kimura, 1968; Jukes and Cantor, 1969) with no observable positive or negative impact. Subsequent studies at the DNA level showed that rate of nucleotide (and of amino acid) substitution is about six- to ten- fold higher in the C peptide, relative to the A and B chains. Historical background: insulin

topics.name: In the 1960s, sequence data were accumulated for small,

chunks.content: abundant proteins such as globins, cytochromes c, and fibrinopeptides. Some proteins appeared to evolve slowly, while others evolved rapidly. Linus Pauling, Emanuel Margoliash and others proposed the hypothesis of a molecular clock: Hypothesis: For every given protein, the rate of molecular evolution is approximately constant in all evolutionary lineages Molecular clock hypothesis

As an example, Richard Dickerson (1971) plotted data from three protein families: cytochrome c, hemoglobin, and fibrinopeptides. The x-axis shows the divergence times of the species, estimated from paleontological data. The y-axis shows m, the corrected number of amino acid changes per 100 residues. n is the observed number of amino acid changes per 100 residues, and it is corrected to m to account for changes that occur but are not observed. Molecular clock hypothesis n 100 = 1 – e -(m/100)
corrected amino acid changes per 100 residues ( m ) Dickerson (1971)

•For each protein, the data lie on a straight line. Thus, the rate of amino acid substitution has remained constant for each protein. •The average rate of change differs for each protein. The time for a 1% change to occur between two lines of evolution is 20 MY (cytochrome c), 5.8 MY (hemoglobin), and 1.1 MY (fibrinopeptides). •The observed variations in rate of change reflect functional constraints imposed by natural selection. Molecular clock hypothesis: conclusions

An often-held view of evolution is that just as organisms propagate through natural selection, so also DNA and protein molecules are selected for. According to Motoo Kimura’s 1968 neutral theory of molecular evolution, the vast majority of DNA changes are not selected for in a Darwinian sense. The main cause of evolutionary change is random drift of mutant alleles that are selectively neutral (or close to neutral). Positive Darwinian selection does occur, but it has a limited role. As an example, the divergent C peptide of insulin changes according to the neutral mutation rate. Neutral theory of evolution

(1978) describe the PAM scoring matrices: “An accepted point mutation in a protein is a replacement of one amino acid by another, accepted by natural selection. It is the result of two distinct processes: the first is the occurrence of a mutation in the portion of the gene template producing one amino acid of a protein; the second is the acceptance of the mutation by the species as the new predominant form. Molecular evolutionary studies can be complicated by the fact that both species and genes evolve. Speciation usually occurs when a species becomes reproductively isolated. In a species tree, each internal node represents a speciation event. Genes (and proteins) may duplicate or otherwise evolve before or after any given speciation event. The topology of a gene (or protein) based tree may differ from the topology of a species tree. Species trees versus gene/protein trees

topics.name: To introduce methods for creating multiple sequence

chunks.content: alignments of nucleotide and protein sequences Primary sources: lecture notes, Pevsner Ch. 6.

• a collection of three or more protein or nucleic acid sequences that are partially or completely aligned • Homologous residues are aligned in columns across the length of the sequences • residues are homologous in an evolutionary sense • residues are homologous in a structural sense

• not necessarily one “correct” alignment of a protein family • protein sequences evolve... • ...the corresponding three-dimensional structures of proteins also evolve (though much more slowly) • may not be possible to identify amino acid residues that align properly throughout a multiple sequence alignment

• some aligned residues, such as cysteines that form disulfide bridges, may be highly conserved • there may be conserved motifs (short signature regions) - e.g. ‘kinase ATP binding’ • often contain conserved secondary structure features • there may be regions with consistent patterns of insertions or deletions (indels)

• MSAs are more sensitive than pairwise alignment to detect homologs •BLAST output can take the form of an MSA, and can reveal conserved residues or motifs •Population data can be analyzed in an MSA (PopSet) •A single query can be searched against a database of MSAs •Regulatory regions of genes may have consensus sequences identifiable by MSA
•MSAs of more distantly related sequences are typically better quality than pairwise alignments

•How many sequences to include? - more is generally better - MSAs of three sequences are of limited use •Removing redundant sequences - highly similar sequences do not add much information - in some cases, it may be useful to choose one representative from a set of similar sequences, e.g. amino acid sequences with >90-95% ID •Removing questionable sequences - sequences need enough similarity to produce a quality alignment ( > 40-50% ID? ) - depending on the goal, some low ID sequences may be included (e.g. sequences of known 3D structure)

•Goal is to include members belonging to the same ‘group’ of proteins •Researcher knowledge of the proteins of interest •what is known about the function of each aligned sequence can help determine if it should be included Other considerations •are the sequences approximately the same length? •is there a known domain structure? •redundant sequences initially removed may be added back to the MSA (e.g. for a specific region of interest) •MSAs may be divided into sub-groups
Progressive alignment (Feng & Doolittle, ClustalW). - sequences are globally aligned in a specified order

local/global weighting (T-coffee) - uses information from other sequences in pairwise alignments - weights locally similar regions Iterative re-alignment (MUSCLE, MAFFT) - sequence subsets removed and re-aligned - uses a tree-based hierarchy Context-based alignment (Decipher) - use secondary structure prediction as part of alignment scores - uses variable gap penalties based on local sequence

topics.name: Clustal-W was one of the first MSA methods. Later methods

chunks.content: have further refinements and often produce better results. [1] Do a set of global pairwise alignments (Needleman and Wunsch) - for N sequences, have N(N-1)/2 alignments [2] Create a guide tree - specifies order of alignment - most similar to least similar [3] Progressively align the sequences - dynamic programming
guide tree, five closely related lipocalins Output is from ClustalW, through EBI server.

•Make a MSA based on the order in the guide tree •Start with the two most closely related sequences •Combine sequences or groups of sequences together based on the order specified in the guide tree •Continue until all sequences are added to the MSA •Rule: “once a gap, always a gap.” - a ‘greedy’ algorithm, no going back to fix things.
combine in order: A B C D E F G 1.C + D 2.A + B 3.AB + CD 4.ABCD + E 5.ABCDE + F 6.ABCDEF + G

•ClustalW produces a guide tree in building the MSA. Can this be used as a phylogenetic tree? •ideally no - it is a tree, but not an optimized phylogenetic tree. •guide tree is based on pairwise similarity, not similarity within the MSA •It’s an intermediate step in analyzing the data for an accurate tree.

• It is possible that a progressive MSA could make an initial error during pairwise alignment • The initial error would be retained in the MSA • Two possible solutions (there are others): - Iteratively remove and re-align sequences within the MSA (e.g. Muscle, MAFFT) - weight alignments using predicted secondary structure, use variable gap costs to optimize gap placement

‘MUltiple Sequence Comparison using Log- Expectation’ 1.estimate a guide tree #1 (their own sequence similarity measure, K-mer counting) 2.Use a progressive alignment to build MSA #1 3.computer percent id for sequence pairs, build new distance matrix 4.compute guide tree #2 based on (3.) 5.build MSA #2 6.delete edges in tree #2 7.compute profiles for two sub-trees 8.realign profiles from 7 to create new MSA 9.If score improves for MSA in 8, keep new MSA 10. Repeat steps 6 to 9 until convergence or limit reached

•Edges are deleted in order of decreasing distance from root of tree •this will effectively re-align each individual sequence to the rest of the MSA •sub-trees of several sequences are also re-aligned, following order in guide tree •accuracy is comparable to other heuristic methods •very fast

•Bioinformatics tools are generally not static and many are updated to improve speed, accuracy, or ease-of-use •MUSCLE had a major update with version 5: Edgar, R.C. chunks.content: matrices, PSSMs are specific to a given multiple sequence alignment, and are often constructed from sequences homologous to an input query sequence. This approach captures patterns of conservation and substitution within a family of related proteins.
•position specific amino acid frequencies are used to refine the scoring function

topics.name: The purpose of PSI-BLAST is to look deeper

chunks.content: into the database for matches to your query protein sequence by employing a scoring matrix that is customized to your query. • based on multiple sequence alignments • residue conservation integrated into scoring

topics.name: PSI-BLAST is performed in five steps

chunks.content: [1] Select a query and search it against a protein database [2] PSI-BLAST constructs a multiple sequence alignment then creates a “profile” or specialized position-specific scoring matrix (PSSM) [3] The PSSM is used as a query against the database [4] PSI-BLAST estimates statistical significance (E values) [5] Repeat steps [3] and [4] iteratively (typically 4-5x). At each new search, a new profile is used as the query.

•for a query of length L, a PSSM of dimensions L x 20 is created. (Later versions expand this to include gaps.) •in iteration #1, rows of the PSSM are essentially the same as in the default matrix (e.g. BLOSUM62) •in later iterations, the frequency of each amino acid at each position in the alignment is weighted and contributes to the score. •redundant sequences (<~94% ID) are listed but not used

topics.name: RPS-BLAST is the inverse operation to PSI-BLAST:

chunks.content: [1] a single sequence is selected as a query [2] The query sequence is compared to an existing database of PSSMs for known protein domains [3] A BLAST-like procedure is used to align the query to PSSMs in the database and generate an alignment score [4] RPS-BLAST estimates statistical significance (E values) No iteration is required, PSSMs are already constructed. RPS-BLAST is the tool used to search the Conserved Domain Database, and is done by default with any BLASTp search. The search time is faster than BLASTp as the database is considerably smaller.

RPS-BLAST example, human p53: This gene encodes a tumor suppressor protein containing transcriptional activation, DNA binding, and oligomerization domains. The encoded protein responds to diverse cellular stresses to regulate expression of target genes, thereby inducing cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, senescence, DNA repair, or changes in metabolism.

topics.name: Delta-BLAST combines PSI-BLAST and RPS-BLAST to identify

chunks.content: remote homologs: [1] a single sequence is selected as a query [2] The query sequence is compared to PSSMs in the Conserved Domain Database [3] A new PSSM for the whole protein is constructed from a multiple alignment of CDD hits [4] The new PSSM is used to search the sequence Database [5] matches are returned and statistical significance is estimated (E values) No iteration is required as CDD hits are already constructed PSSMs. DeltaBLAST is often faster and more accurate than PSI-BLAST
05 Biology 365 Methods in Bioinformatics Lecture 5 – BLAST: A Basic Local Alignment Search Tool Objective: To examine the algorithms and statistics used by BLAST Primary source: Pevsner, Ch. 4.

•Sequence similarity alone is not informative, we often want to deduce function •BLAST is a set of algorithms to compare a query sequence against all sequences in a database •Similarity is measured by aligning two sequences •Each comparison is given a score reflecting degree of similarity, where (higher scores → greater similarity) •The chance (expectation value) that the match could have occurred as a random hit is estimated (smaller E value → more significant, similar to p- values) •Remember similarity does not always equal function! Database searching

•Sequentially align a query sequence to each subject sequence in a database •Results are reported as a ranked list with scores and statistics •‘Heuristic’ (approximate) methods are required due to the huge size of most databases. These are faster than optimal methods such as Smith- Waterman but are not guaranteed to find all results or the best result. E value:  number of random alignments expected to have a score equal or better than the query/subject alignment

allows rapid sequence comparison of a query sequence against a database. The BLAST 2.0 algorithm is fast, accurate, and web- accessible. https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi Applications include • identifying orthologs and paralogs • discovering new genes or proteins • discovering variants of genes or proteins • investigating expressed sequence tags (ESTs) • exploring protein structure and function

It is important to assess the statistical significance

topics.name: The expect value E is the number of alignments with scores

chunks.content: greater than or equal to score S that are expected to occur by chance in a database search. An E value is related to a probability value p, where p = 1 - e - () For values of E and p < 0.05,  E ≈ p. Put another way, E is the expected number of random hits with score ≥ S, and p is the probability of one or more random hits with score ≥ S. How to interpret a BLAST search: expect value

Very small E values are very similar to p values. E values of above 1 are far easier to interpret than corresponding p values. topics.name: Some properties of the equation E = Kmn e

chunks.content: -S • E increases linearly with m and n (search space terms) •The value of E decreases exponentially with increasing S (higher S values correspond to better alignments). Very high scores correspond to very low E values. •The S value for aligning a pair of random sequences must on average be negative! Otherwise, long random alignments would acquire large scores •For E=1, one match with a similar score is expected to occur by chance within a random distribution. For larger or smaller databases, you would expect E to vary accordingly

topics.name: From raw scores to bit scores

chunks.content: • There are two kinds of scores: raw scores (calculated from a substitution matrix) and bit scores (normalized scores) • Bit scores are comparable between different searches because they are normalized to account for the use of different scoring matrices and different database sizes S’ = bit score = (S - lnK) / ln2 The E value corresponding to a given bit score is: E = mn 2 -S’ Bit scores allow you to compare results between different database searches, even using different scoring matrices.

topics.name: Possible changes in amino acid sequence
Observed changes in amino acid sequence are due to

chunks.content: changes in the corresponding nucleotide coding sequence • changes in nucleotide sequence can - result in a change of amino acid - have no effect on AA (synonymous substitution) - result in chain termination or elongation • amino acids are often under functional constraint - the amino acid (or a similar amino acid) is important for the function or structure of the protein - amino acids under functional constraint will have a lower observed rate of change over time

changes in amino acid sequence The observed probability of an amino acid exchange is a function of a number of factors, including: • functional constraint • frequency of amino acid • codon frequency and codon bias • mutability of amino acid - relative functional importance - relative chemical similarity A scoring function can be calculated from observed amino acid exchange rates in homologous proteins, effectively integrating the above factors

A substitution matrix contains values based on the probability that amino acid i mutates to amino acid j for all pairs of amino acids. Substitution matrices are constructed by assembling a large and diverse sample of verified pairwise alignments (or multiple sequence alignments) of amino acids.

topics.name: Substitution matrices should reflect an average of

chunks.content: the probabilities for each possible amino acid change. Two major types of substitution matrices are those based on explicit evolutionary models such as PAM or GTR, or matrices to be used as scoring functions such as BLOSUM. Substitution Matrices

•method of scoring is the same as with nucleotides BLOSUM62 matrix = “BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix 62%” Mouse  I  V  G  G  Y  N  C  E  E  N Crayfish I  V  G  G  T  D  A  V  L  G 4  4  6  6 –2  1  0 -2 –3  0  = 14

•the same optimal alignment methods can be used: - Needleman-Wunsch (global) - Smith-Waterman (local) •Typically match/mismatch scoring is replaced by amino acid specific scores, i.e. (L,L) = 6, (L,V) = 2, (L,D) = -4. •Scores are based on observed frequencies of substitution •Gap penalties are usually based on the origination/extension model (affine gap penalties), though linear gap penalties may also be used.

•Scoring functions are based on probabilistic approach, usually in the form of a comparison between alternate models. •Two standard models used for comparison are i) a non-random (evolutionary) model, and ii) a random model •For any given position in a pairwise alignment, probabilities can be estimated for each model •Models can be compared by the ratio of probabilities, or the odds ratio •Many scoring systems use some variation of a log-odds ratio
•define Q a,b as the probability that an alignment of amino acids a and b occur in an homologous sequence pair (e.g. in the training set)

•define P a,b as the probability of aligning amino acids a and b in a random model, e.g. based on the background probabilities p a and p b for amino acids a and b •the odds ratio for an alignment of a and b in a sequence is Odds ratio = Q a,b P a,b

The first widely used scoring system for aligning proteins, created by Dayhoff et al. (1968, 1978). PAM matrices are based on global alignments of closely related proteins (>85% sequence identity) An accepted point mutation is the replacement of one amino acid by another, and accepted by natural selection Dayhoff et al. constructed a mutation probability matrix M, where element Mij represents the probability of amino acid j being replaced by amino acid i over a given evolutionary interval. For the PAM1 matrix, this interval is equivalent to one change per 100 amino acids. PAM matrices: Point-accepted mutations

•BLOSUM = Blocks Substitution Matrices (Henikoff and Henikoff, 1991, 1992) •Designed for use with database search algorithms •BLOSUM matrices are based on local alignments (developed after the PAM matrices and with more data) •dervied from BLOCKS database, a curated database of a large number of ungapped multiple sequence alignments •differences with PAM matrices: - contain quite dissimilar sequences (still homologous) - sequences are compared directly (no evolutionary model) - intent is to identify conserved regions in sequences - designed for database searches, not models of evolution BLOSUM Scoring

topics.name: All BLOSUM matrices are based on observed alignments;

chunks.content: they are not extrapolated from comparisons of closely related proteins. The BLOCKS database contains thousands of groups of multiple sequence alignments. BLOSUM62 is the default matrix in BLAST 2.0. Though it is tailored for comparisons of moderately distant proteins, it performs well in detecting closer relationships. A search for distant relatives might be more sensitive with a different matrix, but in general BLOSUM62 is the best matrix to use for initial searches Derivation of BLOSUM Matrices

•One potential issue is that similar sequences in the training set will bias results. To address this, sequences were weighted using similarity thresholds •Similar sequences greater than a given threshold (80%, 62% etc.) were clustered into a single sequence. • e.g. for BLOSUM62, no two (averaged) sequences used have more than 62% identity. •Sequence alignment is a fundamental tool of bioinformatics •used to determine if two sequences are evolutionarily related (homologous) • or more precisely, which regions may be homologous •used to identify common domains or motifs between different proteins •can uncover patterns of conservation and variability •useful in genome annotation •Sequence alignment is the identification of residue-residue or nucleotide- nucleotide matches, introducing gaps but preserving the order Sequence Alignments

•Nucleotide sequences (and corresponding protein sequences) can accumulate changes over multiple generations, leading to divergence of sequences within and between species over time •Portions of a genome may get duplicated, and the duplicated regions can accumulate different mutations, additions, or deletions •Genomic fragments can be transferred between different species (common in bacteria) •In most cases it is possible to recognize sequence similarity between related sequences if they are not too divergent Biological basis for sequence similarity

topics.name: Homologs- sequences are from a common ancestral sequence,

chunks.content: e.g. the sequences are related ▪Orthologs - related sequences in different species that have diverged through speciation - whale and horse myoglobin ▪Paralogs - sequences within a genome that share similarity due to duplicated ancestral gene - human alpha and beta chains of hemoglobin ▪Xenologs - relationship due to lateral gene transfer - e.g. some types of antibiotic resistance in bacteria Important concepts: Homology

•Homology is a ‘yes or no’ question, sequences are homologous or they aren’t. The degree of sequence identity (or similarity) between sequences can be used to answer this •percent Identity is the percent of exact matches within a pair of aligned sequences •Similarity includes functionally similar amino acids as approximate matches - e.g. Valine and Isoleucine (hydrophobic) Lysine and Glutamine (charged, hydrophilic) Important concepts: Homology, Identity, Similarity
isoenzymes Human globin genes

“The process of lining up two sequences to achieve maximal levels of identity (and conservation, in the case of amino acid sequences) for the purpose of assessing the degree of similarity and the possibility of homology.” - NCBI glossary Note: It is possible for sequences to appear very similar even if they are not homologous, e.g. as a result of convergent evolution. Typically these are shorter peptide sequences, repeated sequences, or low-complexity regions. Definitions

•Changes that occur through divergence –Substitutions (A to T, T to C etc. or amino acid changes) –Insertions (one or more nucleotides/amino acids) –Deletions (one or more nucleotides/amino acids) •In regions where there is an insertion or deletion (indel event), gaps are inserted

topics.name: Should amino acids or nucleotides be aligned?

chunks.content: Depends on the goal... •protein alignments may be more informative for function •20 amino acids vs. 4 nucleotides (less noise) •many amino acids have related biophysical properties •codon degeneracy: changes in the third position often do not alter the amino acid that is specified •protein sequences offer a longer “look-back” time •DNA sequences can be translated into protein, and then used in pairwise alignments
•in some cases, DNA alignments are more appropriate - to confirm the identity of a cDNA - to study noncoding regions of DNA - to study DNA polymorphisms

•DNA sequences change at a faster rate than protein sequence •DNA may be more informative for closely related species comparisons, or comparisons within a species •when in doubt, compare both Should amino acids or nucleotides be aligned? topics.name: These are two types of sequence alignments
Global:  attempts to align entire sequence
Local:  aligns regions with recognizable similarity

chunks.content: only (regions with score > threshold)

•possible that only a subset of domains share homology •in this case, a local alignment should be used, or subsets of the complete protein sequences selected Many proteins are multi-domain proteins •global alignments can be useful for aligning known domains of low similarity, e.g. to a known structure
•including additional information from other sequences can improve a given pairwise alignment Multiple sequence alignments can improve results

–Can be Exact (will find optimal solution, e.g. best score) or Heuristic (faster, but solution might not be optimal) –Dynamic programming Algorithms - exact •Smith and Waterman (local) •Needleman and Wunsch (global) –Heuristic methods •Word or k-tuple methods (FASTA, BLAST, MegaBLAST, etc) –Graphical methods for visualization (dot matrix)

•Gaps are included to obtain the best possible alignment between two sequences •Insertion and deletion events complicate sequence alignment –e.g. AATCTATA AATCTATA AATCTATA AAG--ATA AA-G-ATA AA--GATA •If scoring an alignment that contains gaps, a “gap penalty” must be included •Penalties are given for each gap introduced (deducted from alignment score) •Rationale is that insertion and mutation events are rare, however they may affect nearby residues and nucleotides •Indels = insertion/deletion events
•a simple score for nucleotides including gap penalty: match=1, mismatch=0, gap penalty=-1
•there may be a number of equally optimal alignments between two sequences •As insertions and deletions are rare, a better alignment may be one with fewer but longer gaps

•It is more likely that the difference in length for 2 sequences over a short region is due to one indel than several (e.g. above sequence) •Thus, we can bias the alignment scoring function to take this into account and gap penalty is divided into two parts -  Gap origination penalty (G O ) -Length (or extension) penalty, G L -Gap penalty = G O + nG L

sequence alignment •Exhaustive alignment methods are not feasible –E.g. two sequences, length 100 and 95, equals ~55 million possible alignments •Dynamic programming algorithms can provide an optimal alignment between two sequences (mathematically proven) •Compares every pair of characters in two sequences and generates an alignment •Matches between identical or related characters are maximized in the alignment •Global alignments: Needleman-Wunsch algorithm (1970) •Local alignments:  Smith-Waterman algorithm (1981)

topics.name: Eddy SR. What is dynamic programming?

chunks.content: Nat Biotechnol. 2004. 22(7):909-10.

•work by breaking big problems into smaller ones •two possible alignments for a pair of sequences: •the first four positions are the same in both. •A dynamic programming algorithm would calculate a score for the first four positions (for example), and use that score as part of the score for the larger alignments. •Saves recalculating the entire score for each alignment SeqA  C  G  G  T  C  T  -  -  A SeqB  -  G  G  C  A  T  C  G  A SeqA  C  G  G  T  -  -  C  T  A SeqB  -  G  G  C  A  T  C  G  A

•uses a two-dimensional array to both calculate and store values for partial sequence alignments. •each entry is the best score for the alignment up to that point •built by filling in array from top to bottom, left to right. •the score to be used here is match=1, mismatch=0, gap=-1 •other values or functions could be used as well, e.g. incorporating gap origination and length penalties CTCG G C T

•a local alignment algorithm, adapted from the Needleman & Wunsch procedure •will recognize regions of local similarity within larger sequences •can also recognize global similarity, if similarity exists (e.g. regions are similar and in the same sequence order) •uses 2D array, similar to Needleman & Wunsch •produces optimal alignments, like N&W •requires a harsher penalty for mismatches, e.g. match = 1, mismatch = -1, gap = -2

•Uses the same basic rules as Needleman & Wunsch - array entry (i,j) is maximum of: [ (i-1, j-1) + (match or mismatch), (i-1, j) + gap penalty, (i, j-1) + gap penalty ] •New rules: - zero is the minimum value for any entry in table - when tracing back through matrix to produce the alignment, start at the maximum value in the table (not lower right corner) - stop traceback when a zero is reached

•Starting point for most bioinformatics research •Storage, sharing and describing of data as well as preliminary analysis through database-associated tools •Simple to very complicated, local to international •Databases are very large and continue to grow •Managing much of this information is the responsibility of large consortiums (NCBI, EBI, DDBJ) •This is now a huge industry in science (and a large employer) •Databases have many different structures. We will deal with: •flat file databases •relational databases
•Data (e.g., sequences) are stored as a text file or a collection of text files •flat, as in a sheet of paper •Easy to input, distribute, search and retrieve data •Examples:

•Most common type of biological database •Data stored within a number of tables linked together by a shared field, the key •a key must be unique to each record •Handles huge amounts of data •reducing data in memory •faster search and retrieval

•Primary vs. derived (secondary) data •primary data = data from experimental results •sequence read data (SRA, sequence read archive) •gene expression data (microarray data, ESTs) •structural data (X-ray, NMR) •derived (secondary) data = results utilizing primary data •conserved protein/domain families •Gene and protein functional annotations •genome assemblies •signal peptides, binding/catalytic sites •many of these annotations are automated

•redundant vs. non-redundant •efficiency (many of the algorithms require processing of a large amount of data, the less duplication the faster the process) •databases are screened to reduce redundancy •Databases can be under automatic and/or manual quality control e.g., UniProtKB consists of: •SWISS-Prot (highly annotated manual component) •TrEMBL (automatically annotated)

•Record may not always be what you are looking for •Computational error –incorrect annotations –missed relationships (insufficient extraction of information) •Human error •Contributions of many different people with different accuracy standards and goals •Fragment of vector sequence included in sequence •PCR chimeras •Taxonomic misidentification •Although most data is accurate, errors are possible
•each of these databases contain equivalent information (formats vary slightly) •data is exchanged daily •we will be using NCBI for retrieving most sequence data

chunks.content: 1 No. Reject source Yes. Evidence for credibility: No conflicts of interest;   Source acknowledged;  Unbiased analysis of topic Does the source have the expertise to vouch for the claim? 2 No. Reject source Yes. Assessing Information in the wild – a quick heuristic Is there a consensus among the relevant scientific experts? 3 No.  Assess the uncertainty: What do the experts agree on and where to they disagree? Hemagglutinin is a major functional protein and immune target.

A major branch of bioinformatics is Clustering and Phylogenetics Clustering – grouping by similarity - could assume changes are additive (changes accumulate along branches) - or, you could assume changes are not additive Phylogenetics – clustering nucleotide or protein sequences by similarity - more detailed than simple clustering - changes are additive - typically follows an evolutionary model - can use different models and assumptions Output of both is a grouping a data points, often as a tree
